questions.question: Events A and B satisfy P(B) > 0. Which expression equals the conditional probability P(A|B)?

explanations.A: Correct. conditioning restricts the world to B, so we take the share of B that also lies in A: P(A ∩ B) / P(B).
explanations.B: P(A)/P(B) ignores the overlap; conditioning needs P(A ∩ B) in the numerator, not P(A).
explanations.C: P(A ∩ B)/P(A) is P(B|A) — the conditioning direction is reversed.
explanations.D: P(A)·P(B) is the joint probability only under independence, not the definition of a conditional.

questions.question: By definition, the conditional probability P(A|B) renormalizes probabilities within which event?
options: B (the conditioning event becomes the new sample space)

explanations.A: We condition on B, not A, so B — not A — becomes the denominator.
explanations.B: Correct. once B is known to occur, B becomes the whole sample space and we measure A inside it.
explanations.C: The union is not the restricted space; knowing B shrinks the space to B alone.
explanations.D: The complement of A is irrelevant to the definition of P(A|B).

questions.question: Of all trading days, P(the market gapped up at the open) = 0.4 and the joint probability P(the market gapped up at the open and the day also closed higher) = 0.12. Given that the market gapped up at the open, what is the probability that the day also closed higher?

explanations.A: 0.12 is the joint probability P(both) before dividing by the conditioning probability.
explanations.B: 0.048 multiplies the two values; conditioning divides by P(condition), it does not multiply.
explanations.C: Correct. P = P(both)/P(condition) = 0.12 / 0.4 = 0.3.
explanations.D: 0.4 is P(condition) itself, not the conditional probability.

questions.question: A single card is drawn and revealed to be a heart. What is P(it is an ace | heart)?

explanations.A: the unconditional chance of the ace of hearts from the full 52, not within hearts
explanations.B: P(heart) for the whole deck, not P(ace) among hearts
explanations.C: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/13.
explanations.D: this counts all four aces but the conditioning leaves only one ace among 13 hearts

questions.question: A card is drawn and shown to be a spade. What is P(it is a face card J/Q/K | spade)?

explanations.A: the unconditional chance of a specific-suit face card, not restricted to spades
explanations.B: P(spade), unrelated to the face-card count inside spades
explanations.C: this counts only one rank; there are three face ranks among the 13 spades
explanations.D: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 3/13.

questions.question: A card is drawn and shown to be red. What is P(it is a heart | red)?

explanations.A: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/2.
explanations.B: P(heart) for the whole deck, not the share of red cards that are hearts
explanations.C: ignores that exactly half of the 26 red cards are hearts
explanations.D: a miscount; hearts make up 13 of the 26 red cards

questions.question: A card is drawn and shown to be a face card (J/Q/K). What is P(it is a king | face card)?

explanations.A: the rank chance in the whole deck, not within the 12 face cards
explanations.B: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/3.
explanations.C: confuses kings-per-suit with the share of kings among face cards
explanations.D: this counts a single specific king; there are four kings among the 12 face cards

questions.question: A fair die is rolled and the result is even. What is P(the die shows 2 | even)?

explanations.A: the unconditional chance of a 2 before learning the roll is even
explanations.B: P(even) itself, not the chance of a specific even face
explanations.C: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/3.
explanations.D: uses the wrong denominator; only three faces (2,4,6) are even

questions.question: A fair die is rolled and the result is greater than 3. What is P(the die shows 5 | result > 3)?

explanations.A: the unconditional chance of a 5 before conditioning on >3
explanations.B: P(result > 3), not the chance of one specific qualifying face
explanations.C: uses 4 outcomes; only {4,5,6} satisfy result > 3
explanations.D: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/3.

questions.question: A fair die is rolled and the result is prime (2, 3, or 5). What is P(the die shows 2 | prime)?

explanations.A: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/3.
explanations.B: the unconditional chance of a 2, not restricted to primes
explanations.C: P(prime) itself, not the chance of one prime face
explanations.D: an invalid count; there are exactly three prime faces

questions.question: Two fair coins are flipped. Given that at least one is heads, what is P(both are heads | at least one head)?

explanations.A: the unconditional chance of two heads before conditioning on at least one
explanations.B: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/3.
explanations.C: ignores that 'at least one head' leaves three equally likely outcomes
explanations.D: inverts the count; only 1 of the 3 surviving outcomes is HH

questions.question: A family has two children. Given that at least one is a boy, what is P(both are boys | at least one boy)?

explanations.A: the unconditional chance of two boys, not the conditional one
explanations.B: the classic intuition trap; three ordered outcomes survive, not two
explanations.C: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/3.
explanations.D: counts the wrong outcome as favorable

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. Given that the sum is even, what is P(both dice are even | sum even)?

explanations.C: overcounts; both-even is exactly half of even-sum outcomes
explanations.D: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/2.

questions.question: A card is drawn and shown to be a club. What is P(it is a number card 2–10 | club)?

explanations.B: the unconditional chance of a specific-suit number card, not within clubs
explanations.C: off by one; ranks 2–10 are nine values, not ten
explanations.D: this is the face+ace count, not the 2–10 number cards

explanations.D: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 1/2.

explanations.C: this is P(red) after removing a blue, not P(blue)
explanations.D: miscounts; 5 blue remain out of 9 after removal

questions.question: A card is drawn and shown to be a number card (2–10). What is P(it is an even number 2/4/6/8/10 | number card)?

explanations.A: ignores that nine ranks (2–10) survive, of which five are even

explanations.C: Correct. restrict to the conditioning event and count favorable outcomes: 5/9.
explanations.D: counts the four odd-then... a miscount; five of nine ranks are even

questions.question: A standard die is rolled and the result is odd. What is P(the die shows 3 | odd)?

questions.question: In general, how do P(A|B) and P(B|A) compare?
options: They are usually different because they divide by different totals, P(B) versus P(A).
They are always equal by symmetry of intersection.
P(A|B) is always the larger of the two.
They are equal only when both equal 0.5.

explanations.A: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.
explanations.B: the intersection is symmetric, but the denominators P(B) and P(A) differ, so the ratios differ
explanations.C: neither is universally larger; it depends on the base rates P(A) and P(B)
explanations.D: equality holds when P(A)=P(B), which has nothing to do with the value being 0.5

questions.question: A test has P(positive | disease) = 0.99. Why might P(disease | positive) still be low?
options: Because sensitivity must be below 0.5 for this to happen.
Because a rare disease means false positives among the many healthy people can outnumber true positives.
Because Bayes' theorem does not apply to medical tests.

explanations.A: high sensitivity is fine; the low posterior comes from the small base rate
explanations.B: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.
explanations.C: specificity is typically high; the driver is the rare prior, not zero specificity
explanations.D: medical testing is a textbook Bayes application; the theorem absolutely applies

questions.question: A prosecutor argues 'the chance of this DNA match if innocent is 1 in a million, so the chance the defendant is innocent is 1 in a million.' This confuses:
options: the prior with the likelihood, which are actually identical.
P(evidence | innocent) with P(innocent | evidence) — the two conditionals are different.
the union with the intersection of events.

explanations.A: priors and likelihoods are not identical; conflating the two directions is the error
explanations.B: the fallacy is about reversing a conditional, not about independence versus exclusivity
explanations.C: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.
explanations.D: no union/intersection mix-up is involved; it is a reversed-conditional error

questions.question: It is cloudy on 50% of days and rains on 30% of days, with P(cloudy | rain) = 0.9. Which quantity is generally NOT equal to P(rain | cloudy)?

explanations.A: this is exactly the definition of P(rain | cloudy)
explanations.B: this is Bayes' theorem giving P(rain | cloudy) correctly
explanations.C: intersection is commutative, so this also equals P(rain | cloudy)
explanations.D: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.

questions.question: P(A|B) equals P(B|A) precisely when:
options: P(A) = P(B) (the two events have equal marginal probability).
Never, the two are always different.
Only when A and B are mutually exclusive.

explanations.A: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.
explanations.B: the shared numerator is symmetric, but equal conditionals require equal denominators P(A)=P(B)
explanations.C: they coincide whenever P(A)=P(B), so 'never' is wrong
explanations.D: mutual exclusivity forces both to 0, a degenerate case, not the general condition

questions.question: Most people overestimate P(disease | positive) by treating it as equal to P(positive | disease). This mistake is called:
options: the base-rate fallacy — neglecting the prior probability of the disease.

explanations.A: the gambler's fallacy is about independent trials, not reversed conditionals
explanations.B: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.
explanations.C: the conjunction fallacy concerns P(A and B) > P(A); it is a different error
explanations.D: sunk cost is a decision bias, unrelated to conditional probability direction

questions.question: If P(spam | contains 'free') = 0.8, what can we conclude about P(contains 'free' | spam)?
options: Nothing directly — reversing the conditional requires the base rates P(spam) and P('free').

explanations.A: the reversed conditional is generally different unless the marginals are equal
explanations.B: 0.2 is the complement of the given conditional, not the reversed one
explanations.C: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.
explanations.D: no such ordering holds; the value depends on the marginal probabilities

questions.question: A spam filter has high P(flag | spam) but users still see spam in the inbox. The relevant inbox quality measure is:
options: P(flag | spam), which is what we are already told.
P(spam), the overall rate ignoring the filter.
P(flag), the fraction of all mail flagged.
P(spam | not flagged) — the conditioning direction users actually experience.

explanations.A: that is detection on known spam, not the inbox-quality conditional users care about
explanations.B: the marginal ignores the filter's effect on what reaches the inbox
explanations.C: this measures flagging volume, not the chance unflagged mail is spam
explanations.D: Correct. the two conditioning directions use different denominators, so they are not interchangeable here.

questions.question: Let B₁,…,Bₙ be a partition (mutually exclusive and exhaustive). The law of total probability says P(A) equals:

explanations.A: Correct. A occurs through exactly one scenario Bᵢ, so sum the conditional pieces weighted by how likely each scenario is.
explanations.B: this reverses the conditioning and repeats P(A); it is not the total-probability formula
explanations.C: summing conditionals without the weights P(Bᵢ) ignores how likely each scenario is
explanations.D: multiplying conditionals applies to independent chains, not to a partition decomposition

questions.question: For a partition B₁,…,Bₙ (disjoint and covering the sample space), the sum P(B₁)+…+P(Bₙ) equals:

explanations.A: a partition's probabilities cannot all vanish; they must cover the whole space
explanations.B: Correct. an exhaustive set of disjoint events accounts for every outcome, so their probabilities sum to 1.
explanations.C: the partition sum is fixed at 1 and does not depend on any event A
explanations.D: summing probabilities (each ≤ 1) gives 1, not the count of pieces

questions.question: Suppose P(A signal is bullish) = 0.4. When A signal is bullish, P(the stock rises) = 0.25; otherwise P(the stock rises) = 0.5. What is the overall P(the stock rises)?

explanations.A: 0.25 is the conditional rate in one scenario only, not the weighted total.
explanations.B: 0.5 is the conditional rate in the other scenario only.
explanations.D: 0.375 averages the two rates without weighting by how likely each scenario is.

questions.question: Suppose P(The economy is in recession) = 0.3. When The economy is in recession, P(a firm defaults) = 0.6; otherwise P(a firm defaults) = 0.2. What is the overall P(a firm defaults)?

explanations.A: 0.6 is the conditional rate in one scenario only, not the weighted total.
explanations.B: 0.2 is the conditional rate in the other scenario only.
explanations.C: 0.4 averages the two rates without weighting by how likely each scenario is.

questions.question: Suppose P(A part comes from the day shift) = 0.5. When A part comes from the day shift, P(it is defective) = 0.8; otherwise P(it is defective) = 0.2. What is the overall P(it is defective)?

explanations.B: 0.8 is the conditional rate in one scenario only, not the weighted total.
explanations.C: 0.2 is the conditional rate in the other scenario only.

questions.question: Two urns are equally likely. Urn A draws red with probability 3/5; Urn B draws red with probability 1/5. A red ball is drawn. What is P(it came from Urn A | red)?

explanations.C: Correct. equal priors cancel: P(A|red) = P(red|A) / [P(red|A)+P(red|B)] = 3/5 / 4/5 = 3/4.
explanations.D: 1/4 is P(Urn B | red), the complementary posterior.

questions.question: A plant runs Line 1 (7/10 of output, defect rate 1/100) and Line 2 (3/10 of output, defect rate 1/10). A randomly inspected unit is defective. What is P(it came from Line 2 | defective)?

questions.question: P(rain tomorrow) = 3/10. The forecast calls rain with P(forecast rain | rain) = 17/20 and P(forecast rain | no rain) = 1/5. The forecast calls for rain. What is P(it actually rains | forecast rain)?
